segments.text: MEMBRANE PROTEINS

MEMBRANE COMPONENTS
Lipid bilayer: basic structure of membrane (isolation from environment)
Proteins: receptors, transport channels, enzymes, etc. (i.e., do the “work”)

MEMBRANE PROTEINS
2 types
integral – embedded in hydrophobic middle of membrane
peripheral – attached to surface of membrane

INTEGRAL MEMBRANE PROTEINS
Types of attachment
a-helices (single pass, multi-pass, monolayer)
b-barrels
non-protein anchors

NON-PROTEIN ANCHORS
Hydrophobic molecule covalently attached to protein

TRANSMEMBRANE SEGMENTS
~20 amino acids for a-helix to span hydrophobic core
segment rich in hydrophobic amino acids

HYDROPATHY PLOTS
Allow prediction of membrane a-helices

HYDROPATHY PLOT CALCULATIONS
Average hydrophobicity of amino acids 1-20 = one point on graph
Repeat for 2-21, 3-22, etc

PERIPHERAL MEMBRANE PROTEINS
Attached through ionic bonds with integral membrane proteins or lipids

GLYCOPROTEINS
Oligosaccharides (sugars) added covalently
Function in cell identity
Sugars only on non-cytosolic side

CHARACTERIZING MEMBRANE PROTEINS
How many proteins present?
Integral vs. peripheral
Peripheral – internal vs. external
Integral – internal vs. external vs. membrane domains

HOW MANY PROTEINS?
Disrupt membrane > wash out interior > allow membrane to reseal
Dissolve lipids and proteins with SDS and bME
Run samples on gel
Proteins separate by size only

INTEGRAL VS. PERIPHERAL
Isolate “washed out” vesicles
Incubate in “high salt” buffer (e.g., 0.6 M NaCl)
Centrifuge at high-speed
Vesicles in pellet
Salt-stripped proteins (external peripheral) in supernatant
Run pellet and supernatant on gel

INTERNAL VS. EXTERNAL PERIPHERAL
Isolated vesicles are normally right side out (ROVs)
Inside out vesicles (IOVs) created with freeze-thaw cycles
To separate internal peripheral proteins, repeat salt stripping with IOVs

INTEGRAL MEMBRANE PROTEIN DOMAINS
Protease cocktail: entirely degrades accessible domains
Proteases: large proteins that cannot cross the membrane (only external domains degraded)
Treat ROVs and IOVs with protease cocktail > run on gel

STUDYING INTEGRAL MEMBRANE PROTEINS
Problem: hydrophobic regions not H2O soluble
Solution: detergents
Detergent: amphipathic molecule able to dissolve hydrophobic regions within aqueous solution
PURIFYING INTEGRAL MEMBRANE PROTEINS

MEMBRANE FLUIDITY
Frye and Edidin (1970)

STUDYING MEMBRANE PROTEIN FLUIDITY
FRAP assay: same as with lipid movement but fluorescently label protein

FLUID MOSAIC MODEL
Current model for plasma membrane
Fluid – molecules move
Mosaic – made of lipids and proteins
Singer and Nicolson (1972)

RESTRICTED PROTEIN MOVEMENT
Not all proteins move freely
Many restricted to specific locations (e.g., sperm membrane proteins)

METHODS OF RESTRICTING MOBILITY
Large aggregates (A)
Interaction with other molecules outside (B) or inside (C) cell
Interaction with surface proteins of adjacent cell (D)

CYTOSKELETAL DEFINED DOMAINS
Restrict movement of membrane proteins
Proteins can escape domains (but rarely)
